title: The Address That No Longer Exists
text_zh: 我的身份证上写着一个已经不存在的地址。十二年前那条街被拆迁了，原来的平房区变成了一片高档商业综合体，但我的户籍地址因为种种行政原因一直没有更新。每次办理正式文件需要填写家庭住址的时候，我都会写下那个虚幻的地址，感觉像是在给一个已经死去的地方写信。有时候我会特意去那个地址的旧址走一走，试图在崭新的玻璃幕墙和大理石地板上辨认出童年的痕迹。入口处的那棵老槐树没有了，取而代之的是一个精心修剪的灌木丛。我们家门前那条石板路消失在了厚厚的柏油层下面。曾经的邻居们被分散安置到了城市的各个角落，偶尔在微信群里分享旧照片和共同的回忆。我逐渐意识到，一个地址不仅仅是一组空间坐标，它是你与一段人生经历之间的锚点。当这个锚点被物理性地移除以后，那些与之相关的记忆就变得像是漂浮的岛屿——存在着，但不再有固定的位置。
text_pinyin: Wǒ de shēnfènzhèng shàng xiě zhe yí gè yǐjīng bù cúnzài de dìzhǐ. Shí'èr nián qián nà tiáo jiē bèi chāiqiān le, yuánlái de píngfáng qū biàn chéng le yí piàn gāodàng shāngyè zōnghétǐ, dàn wǒ de hùjí dìzhǐ yīnwèi zhǒngzhǒng xíngzhèng yuányīn yìzhí méiyǒu gēngxīn. Měi cì bànlǐ zhèngshì wénjiàn xūyào tiánxiě jiātíng zhùzhǐ de shíhou, wǒ dōu huì xiě xià nàge xūhuàn de dìzhǐ, gǎnjué xiàng shì zài gěi yí gè yǐjīng sǐqù de dìfang xiě xìn. Yǒushíhou wǒ huì tèyì qù nàge dìzhǐ de jiùzhǐ zǒu yi zǒu, shìtú zài zhǎnxīn de bōli mùqiáng hé dàlǐshí dìbǎn shàng biànrèn chū tóngnián de hénjì. Rùkǒu chù de nà kē lǎo huáishù méiyǒu le, qǔ ér dài zhī de shì yí gè jīngxīn xiūjiǎn de guànmù cóng. Wǒmen jiā ménqián nà tiáo shíbǎn lù xiāoshī zài le hòuhòu de bǎiyóu céng xiàmiàn. Céngjīng de línjūmen bèi fēnsàn ānzhì dào le chéngshì de gè gè jiǎoluò, ǒu'ěr zài Wēixìn qún lǐ fēnxiǎng jiù zhàopiàn hé gòngtóng de huíyì. Wǒ zhújiàn yìshí dào, yí gè dìzhǐ bùjǐnjǐn shì yì zǔ kōngjiān zuòbiāo, tā shì nǐ yǔ yí duàn rénshēng jīnglì zhī jiān de máodiǎn. Dāng zhège máodiǎn bèi wùlǐxìng de yíchú yǐhòu, nàxiē yǔ zhī xiāngguān de jìyì jiù biàn de xiàng shì piāofú de dǎoyǔ — cúnzài zhe, dàn bú zài yǒu gùdìng de wèizhì.
text_en: My ID card bears an address that no longer exists. Twelve years ago that street was demolished; the original low-rise housing area became an upscale commercial complex, but my registered address was never updated due to various administrative reasons. Every time I need to fill in a home address for official documents, I write down that phantom address, feeling as though I'm writing a letter to a place that has died. Sometimes I deliberately visit the old site, trying to discern childhood traces amid the brand-new glass curtain walls and marble floors. The old scholar tree at the entrance is gone, replaced by a meticulously trimmed bush. The stone-slab path in front of our house disappeared beneath a thick layer of asphalt. Former neighbors were dispersed to various corners of the city, occasionally sharing old photos and shared memories in a WeChat group. I gradually realized that an address is not merely a set of spatial coordinates — it's the anchor between you and a chapter of life experience. When this anchor is physically removed, the memories associated with it become like floating islands — they exist, but no longer have a fixed position.

questions.q_zh: 作者的身份证地址为什么没有更新？
questions.q_en: Why hasn't the author's ID address been updated?

options.text_en: Because the author doesn't want to update it

options.pinyin: yīnwèi xīn dìzhǐ hái méi quèdìng
options.text_en: Because the new address hasn't been determined
Because the processing fee is too expensive

questions.q_zh: 作者认为地址的本质是什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author believe the essence of an address is?

options.text: 你与一段人生经历之间的锚点
options.pinyin: nǐ yǔ yí duàn rénshēng jīnglì zhī jiān de máodiǎn
options.text_en: An anchor between you and a chapter of life experience

title: The Profession I Explain Differently to Everyone
title_zh: 对每个人解释不同的职业
text_zh: 我的职业是用户体验设计师，但根据交谈对象的不同，我对这份工作的描述会发生根本性的变化。对父母说的时候，我简化为「在电脑上画图的」，因为任何更详细的解释都只会让他们更困惑。对亲戚们说的时候，我会说「在互联网公司上班」，然后迅速把话题转到他们能理解的领域——薪水待遇和加班情况。对不太熟的朋友，我用「设计手机应用界面的」来概括，这通常能终结他们的追问。只有在行业聚会上，我才能使用完整的专业术语来描述自己的工作，而不必担心对方听不懂。这种针对不同听众的翻译过程让我意识到，职业身份不是一个固定的标签，而是一组根据社交场景不断调整的叙事策略。每一种描述都是真实的，但每一种都只展示了全貌的一个切面。最有趣的是当有人问「你具体做什么」的时候，我发现自己真正纠结的不是如何解释工作内容，而是在这个场景下，对方希望了解的是哪个层面的「我」。
text_pinyin: Wǒ de zhíyè shì yònghù tǐyàn shèjìshī, dàn gēnjù jiāotán duìxiàng de bùtóng, wǒ duì zhè fèn gōngzuò de miáoshù huì fāshēng gēnběnxìng de biànhuà. Duì fùmǔ shuō de shíhou, wǒ jiǎnhuà wéi 「zài diànnǎo shàng huà tú de」, yīnwèi rènhé gèng xiángxì de jiěshì dōu zhǐ huì ràng tāmen gèng kùnhuò. Duì qīnqimen shuō de shíhou, wǒ huì shuō 「zài hùliánwǎng gōngsī shàngbān」, ránhòu xùnsù bǎ huàtí zhuǎn dào tāmen néng lǐjiě de lǐngyù — xīnshuǐ dàiyù hé jiābān qíngkuàng. Duì bú tài shú de péngyou, wǒ yòng 「shèjì shǒujī yìngyòng jièmiàn de」 lái gàikuò, zhè tōngcháng néng zhōngjié tāmen de zhuīwèn. Zhǐ yǒu zài hángyè jùhuì shàng, wǒ cái néng shǐyòng wánzhěng de zhuānyè shùyǔ lái miáoshù zìjǐ de gōngzuò, ér bùbì dānxīn duìfāng tīng bù dǒng. Zhè zhǒng zhēnduì bùtóng tīngzhòng de fānyì guòchéng ràng wǒ yìshí dào, zhíyè shēnfèn búshì yí gè gùdìng de biāoqiān, ér shì yì zǔ gēnjù shèjiāo chǎngjǐng búduàn tiáozhěng de xùshì cèlüè. Měi yì zhǒng miáoshù dōu shì zhēnshí de, dàn měi yì zhǒng dōu zhǐ zhǎnshì le quánmào de yí gè qiēmiàn. Zuì yǒuqù de shì dāng yǒu rén wèn 「nǐ jùtǐ zuò shénme」 de shíhou, wǒ fāxiàn zìjǐ zhēnzhèng jiūjié de búshì rúhé jiěshì gōngzuò nèiróng, ér shì zài zhège chǎngjǐng xià, duìfāng xīwàng liǎojiě de shì nǎge céngmiàn de 「wǒ」.
text_en: My profession is UX designer, but depending on who I'm talking to, my description of this work undergoes fundamental changes. To my parents, I simplify it to 'someone who draws on a computer,' because any more detailed explanation would only confuse them further. To relatives, I say 'I work at an internet company,' then swiftly redirect the conversation to territory they can comprehend — salary and overtime. To casual acquaintances, I summarize with 'I design mobile app interfaces,' which usually ends their follow-up questions. Only at industry gatherings can I use complete professional terminology to describe my work without worrying about incomprehension. This translation process for different audiences made me realize that professional identity isn't a fixed label, but a set of narrative strategies constantly adjusted for social contexts. Each description is truthful, yet each reveals only one cross-section of the full picture. Most interesting is when someone asks 'what exactly do you do' — I find what I truly struggle with isn't how to explain the work content, but which layer of 'me' the other person hopes to understand in this particular context.

questions.q_zh: 作者对父母怎么解释自己的工作？
questions.q_en: How does the author explain their work to parents?

options.pinyin: zài diànnǎo shàng huà tú de
options.text_en: Someone who draws on a computer

questions.q_zh: 作者认为职业身份的本质是什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author consider the nature of professional identity?

options.text: 根据社交场景调整的叙事策略
options.pinyin: gēnjù shèjiāo chǎngjǐng tiáozhěng de xùshì cèlüè
options.text_en: Narrative strategies adjusted for social contexts
The title on a business card

title: The Haircut That Changed Everything
text_zh: 二十七岁那年我做了一个重大决定：把留了五年的长发剪短。当时我坐在理发椅上，看着镜子里的自己，感觉即将要和一个旧版本的自我告别。那个留着长发的「我」是一个刻意追求与众不同的年轻人，把发型当作一种无声的叛逆宣言。当第一缕头发落到地上的时候，我感到一阵奇怪的轻松——不仅仅是物理上的减轻，更像是一种心理上的卸载。短发的我看起来更加普通了，走在街上不会再引起侧目。但奇怪的是，正是这种「普通」给了我一种前所未有的自由感。我不再需要通过外表来证明自己的独特性，因为我终于意识到真正的独特性从来不是视觉上的。理发师问我要不要把剪下来的头发留作纪念，我想了想说不用了。那些头发代表的那个阶段的我已经完成了它的使命——教会我如何先成为别人眼中的自己，然后再学会放弃那个角色，回到真正的自己。
text_pinyin: Èrshíqī suì nà nián wǒ zuò le yí gè zhòngdà juédìng: bǎ liú le wǔ nián de cháng fà jiǎn duǎn. Dāngshí wǒ zuò zài lǐfà yǐ shàng, kàn zhe jìngzi lǐ de zìjǐ, gǎnjué jíjiāng yào hé yí gè jiù bǎnběn de zìwǒ gàobié. Nàge liú zhe cháng fà de 「wǒ」 shì yí gè kèyì zhuīqiú yǔ zhòng bùtóng de niánqīngrén, bǎ fàxíng dāng zuò yì zhǒng wúshēng de pànnì xuānyán. Dāng dì yī lǚ tóufa luò dào dì shàng de shíhou, wǒ gǎndào yí zhèn qíguài de qīngsōng — bùjǐnjǐn shì wùlǐ shàng de jiǎnqīng, gèng xiàng shì yì zhǒng xīnlǐ shàng de xièzài. Duǎn fà de wǒ kàn qǐlái gèngjiā pǔtōng le, zǒu zài jiē shàng bú huì zài yǐnqǐ cèmù. Dàn qíguài de shì, zhèngshì zhè zhǒng 「pǔtōng」 gěi le wǒ yì zhǒng qiánsuǒwèiyǒu de zìyóu gǎn. Wǒ bú zài xūyào tōngguò wàibiǎo lái zhèngmíng zìjǐ de dútèxìng, yīnwèi wǒ zhōngyú yìshí dào zhēnzhèng de dútèxìng cónglái búshì shìjué shàng de. Lǐfà shī wèn wǒ yào bú yào bǎ jiǎn xiàlái de tóufa liú zuò jìniàn, wǒ xiǎng le xiǎng shuō bú yòng le. Nàxiē tóufa dàibiǎo de nàge jiēduàn de wǒ yǐjīng wánchéng le tā de shǐmìng — jiāohuì wǒ rúhé xiān chéngwéi biérén yǎn zhōng de zìjǐ, ránhòu zài xuéhuì fàngqì nàge juésè, huí dào zhēnzhèng de zìjǐ.
text_en: At twenty-seven I made a major decision: to cut short the long hair I'd kept for five years. Sitting in the barber's chair, looking at myself in the mirror, I felt I was about to say goodbye to an old version of myself. The long-haired 'me' was a young person deliberately pursuing distinctiveness, treating hairstyle as a silent declaration of rebellion. When the first strand of hair fell to the floor, I felt a strange lightness — not merely physical relief, but more like a psychological unloading. The short-haired me looked more ordinary, no longer drawing sideways glances on the street. But strangely, it was precisely this 'ordinariness' that gave me an unprecedented sense of freedom. I no longer needed to prove my uniqueness through appearance, because I finally realized that true uniqueness was never visual. The barber asked if I wanted to keep the cut hair as a memento; I thought about it and said no. That phase of me represented by that hair had completed its mission — teaching me how to first become the self others see, and then learning to relinquish that role and return to the real self.

questions.q_zh: 剪完头发以后作者有什么感受？
questions.q_en: How did the author feel after the haircut?

options.text: 一种物理和心理上的轻松和自由感
options.pinyin: yì zhǒng wùlǐ hé xīnlǐ shàng de qīngsōng hé zìyóu gǎn
options.text_en: Physical and psychological lightness and freedom

questions.q_zh: 作者认为真正的独特性是什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author believe true uniqueness is?

title: The Face in the Old Passport
text_zh: 在书架最高层的一个铁盒子里，我保存着三本过期的护照。把它们按时间顺序排列，就像翻开一本用官方证件照讲述的视觉编年史。第一本护照是十八岁办的，照片上的男孩面无表情，眼神却透着一种急切的期待——那是一个即将第一次出国的年轻人对未知世界的渴望。第二本护照拍摄于二十六岁，这张照片明显成熟了很多：下巴线条更清晰了，眼神里的急切被一种冷静的自信取代，嘴角有了一个微妙的弧度——不是微笑，而是一种「我知道镜头在看我」的自觉。第三本护照是三十四岁的，也是变化最大的一张。额头出现了隐约的纹路，眼角有了细纹，但最大的变化在眼神里：它变得柔和了，不再试图证明什么，也不再掩饰什么。我发现证件照的有趣之处在于它的严格限制——不许笑、不许戴眼镜、不许化妆——反而迫使一张脸展示出最本真的状态。没有表情管理的干预，脸就成了时间最诚实的画布。
text_pinyin: Zài shūjià zuì gāo céng de yí gè tiě hézi lǐ, wǒ bǎocún zhe sān běn guòqī de hùzhào. Bǎ tāmen àn shíjiān shùnxù páiliè, jiù xiàng fān kāi yì běn yòng guānfāng zhèngjiàn zhào jiǎngshù de shìjué biānniánshǐ. Dì yī běn hùzhào shì shíbā suì bàn de, zhàopiàn shàng de nánhái miàn wú biǎoqíng, yǎnshén què tòu zhe yì zhǒng jíqiè de qīdài — nà shì yí gè jíjiāng dì yī cì chūguó de niánqīngrén duì wèizhī shìjiè de kěwàng. Dì èr běn hùzhào pāishè yú èrshíliù suì, zhè zhāng zhàopiàn míngxiǎn chéngshú le hěn duō: xiàba xiàntiáo gèng qīngxī le, yǎnshén lǐ de jíqiè bèi yì zhǒng lěngjìng de zìxìn qǔdài, zuǐjiǎo yǒu le yí gè wēimiào de húdù — búshì wēixiào, ér shì yì zhǒng 「wǒ zhīdào jìngtóu zài kàn wǒ」 de zìjué. Dì sān běn hùzhào shì sānshísì suì de, yě shì biànhuà zuì dà de yì zhāng. Étóu chūxiàn le yǐnyuē de wénlù, yǎnjiǎo yǒu le xìwén, dàn zuì dà de biànhuà zài yǎnshén lǐ: tā biàn de róuhé le, bú zài shìtú zhèngmíng shénme, yě bú zài yǎnshì shénme. Wǒ fāxiàn zhèngjiàn zhào de yǒuqù zhī chù zàiyú tā de yángé xiànzhì — bù xǔ xiào, bù xǔ dài yǎnjìng, bù xǔ huàzhuāng — fǎn'ér pòshǐ yì zhāng liǎn zhǎnshì chū zuì běnzhēn de zhuàngtài. Méiyǒu biǎoqíng guǎnlǐ de gānyù, liǎn jiù chéng le shíjiān zuì chéngshí de huàbù.
text_en: In a tin box on the highest shelf of my bookcase, I keep three expired passports. Arranged chronologically, they read like a visual chronicle told through official ID photos. The first passport was obtained at eighteen; the boy in the photo is expressionless, yet his eyes betray an eager anticipation — the longing of a young person about to travel abroad for the first time. The second passport was taken at twenty-six; this photo is noticeably more mature: the jawline sharper, the eagerness in the eyes replaced by calm confidence, the corner of the mouth carrying a subtle curve — not a smile, but an awareness of 'I know the camera is watching me.' The third passport is from age thirty-four, the one with the biggest changes. Faint lines have appeared on the forehead, fine wrinkles at the eye corners, but the greatest change is in the eyes: they've become gentle, no longer trying to prove anything, nor to conceal anything. I've found that the interesting thing about ID photos is their strict restrictions — no smiling, no glasses, no makeup — which paradoxically force a face to reveal its most authentic state. Without the intervention of expression management, the face becomes time's most honest canvas.

questions.q_zh: 第三本护照上的照片最大的变化是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the biggest change in the third passport photo?

questions.q_zh: 作者认为证件照的限制有什么有趣的效果？
questions.q_en: What interesting effect do the author think ID photo restrictions have?

options.pinyin: ràng rén kàn qǐlái gèng chǒu

options.text: 迫使脸展示出最本真的状态
options.pinyin: pòshǐ liǎn zhǎnshì chū zuì běnzhēn de zhuàngtài
options.text_en: Forcing the face to show its most authentic state

options.text: 让所有照片看起来一样
options.pinyin: ràng suǒyǒu zhàopiàn kàn qǐlái yíyàng
options.text_en: Making all photos look the same

title: The Shoes That Remember
text_zh: 我有一双穿了七年的旧皮鞋，鞋底磨损的形状忠实地记录了我的行走习惯。右脚鞋底的外侧磨损得更厉害，说明我走路时右脚有轻微的外翻。左脚的鞋跟比右脚磨得更快，这是因为我等红灯的时候习惯性地用左脚支撑身体。鞋面上也留下了各种痕迹：左脚背上有一道浅浅的划痕，是两年前骑自行车时踏板刮到的；右脚的鞋带孔附近有一小块褪色的区域，那是去年夏天不小心溅到了漂白剂。每次想扔掉这双鞋的时候，我都会犹豫。不是因为舍不得花钱买新鞋，而是因为一双新鞋需要很长时间才能变成「我的鞋」——鞋垫需要适应我的脚型，鞋帮需要在我的行走中逐渐软化，鞋底需要被我的步态重新雕刻。一双真正属于你的鞋子不是买来的，而是走出来的。它是你身体的延伸，是你与地面之间无数次对话的物质记录。穿着别人的新鞋不会不舒服，但永远不会有那种「刚好」的感觉。
text_pinyin: Wǒ yǒu yì shuāng chuān le qī nián de jiù píxié, xiédǐ mósǔn de xíngzhuàng zhōngshí de jìlù le wǒ de xíngzǒu xíguàn. Yòu jiǎo xiédǐ de wàicè mósǔn de gèng lìhai, shuōmíng wǒ zǒulù shí yòu jiǎo yǒu qīngwēi de wàifān. Zuǒ jiǎo de xiégēn bǐ yòu jiǎo mó de gèng kuài, zhè shì yīnwèi wǒ děng hóngdēng de shíhou xíguànxìng de yòng zuǒ jiǎo zhīchēng shēntǐ. Xiémiàn shàng yě liú xià le gè zhǒng hénjì: zuǒ jiǎo bèi shàng yǒu yí dào qiǎnqiǎn de huáhén, shì liǎng nián qián qí zìxíngchē shí tàbǎn guā dào de; yòu jiǎo de xiédài kǒng fùjìn yǒu yì xiǎo kuài tuìsè de qūyù, nà shì qùnián xiàtiān bù xiǎoxīn jiàn dào le piǎobáijì. Měi cì xiǎng rēng diào zhè shuāng xié de shíhou, wǒ dōu huì yóuyù. Búshì yīnwèi shěbude huā qián mǎi xīn xié, ér shì yīnwèi yì shuāng xīn xié xūyào hěn cháng shíjiān cái néng biàn chéng 「wǒ de xié」 — xiédiàn xūyào shìyìng wǒ de jiǎo xíng, xiébāng xūyào zài wǒ de xíngzǒu zhōng zhújiàn ruǎnhuà, xiédǐ xūyào bèi wǒ de bùtài chóngxīn diāokè. Yì shuāng zhēnzhèng shǔyú nǐ de xiézi búshì mǎi lái de, ér shì zǒu chūlái de. Tā shì nǐ shēntǐ de yánshēn, shì nǐ yǔ dìmiàn zhī jiān wúshù cì duìhuà de wùzhì jìlù. Chuān zhe biérén de xīn xié bú huì bù shūfu, dàn yǒngyuǎn bú huì yǒu nà zhǒng 「gānghǎo」 de gǎnjué.
text_en: I have a pair of old leather shoes I've worn for seven years; the pattern of sole wear faithfully records my walking habits. The outside of the right sole is more worn, indicating my right foot has a slight outward roll when walking. The left heel wears faster than the right because I habitually support my body on my left foot while waiting at red lights. The uppers also bear various marks: a shallow scratch on the left instep from a bicycle pedal scrape two years ago; a small faded patch near the right shoe's lace holes from accidentally splashing bleach last summer. Every time I think of discarding these shoes, I hesitate. Not because I grudge buying new ones, but because a new pair takes a long time to become 'my shoes' — the insole needs to adapt to my foot shape, the upper needs to gradually soften through my walking, the sole needs to be re-sculpted by my gait. Shoes that truly belong to you aren't bought — they're walked into being. They are extensions of your body, material records of countless conversations between you and the ground. Wearing someone else's new shoes won't be uncomfortable, but will never have that feeling of 'just right.'

questions.q_zh: 作者的鞋底磨损说明了什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author's sole wear pattern indicate?

options.text: 作者走路时右脚有轻微的外翻
options.pinyin: zuòzhě zǒulù shí yòu jiǎo yǒu qīngwēi de wàifān
options.text_en: The author's right foot has a slight outward roll when walking

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么舍不得扔掉旧鞋？
questions.q_en: Why is the author reluctant to throw away the old shoes?

options.pinyin: yīnwèi mǎi xīn xié tài guì le
options.text_en: Because new shoes are too expensive

options.text: 因为新鞋需要很长时间才能变成「我的鞋」
options.pinyin: yīnwèi xīn xié xūyào hěn cháng shíjiān cái néng biàn chéng 「wǒ de xié」
options.text_en: Because new shoes take a long time to become 'my shoes'

options.text: 因为这是朋友送的礼物
options.pinyin: yīnwèi zhè shì péngyou sòng de lǐwù
options.text_en: Because they were a gift from a friend

options.pinyin: yīnwèi mǎi bú dào tóng kuǎn
options.text_en: Because the same style is unavailable

title: The Playlist as Self-Portrait
text_zh: 如果有人想真正了解我，我会建议他们不要看我的社交媒体主页，而是去翻我手机上的音乐播放列表。那里面有将近八百首歌，按照添加时间排列，构成了一部比任何日记都更诚实的情感编年史。大学时期添加的歌曲充满了愤怒和理想主义——摇滚乐、朋克、带有社会批判色彩的民谣。二十五岁到二十八岁之间的歌单几乎全是英文歌，那是我刻意拓展审美边界的阶段。三十岁以后的歌单开始回归中文歌曲，而且类型逐渐集中到了轻音乐和爵士——我猜这大概就是人们所说的「口味变清淡了」。最私密的是那些被标记为「深夜专用」的歌单，里面的曲目选择暴露了我在理性表面之下的脆弱——那些在凌晨两点单曲循环播放的歌，每一首都对应着一个不愿意向任何人提起的时刻。我有时候会想，如果我去世以后有人打开我的歌单，他们能不能仅仅通过音乐的轨迹还原一个完整的我？也许不能，但他们至少能触摸到那些我从未说出口的情绪。
text_pinyin: Rúguǒ yǒu rén xiǎng zhēnzhèng liǎojiě wǒ, wǒ huì jiànyì tāmen búyào kàn wǒ de shèjiāo méitǐ zhǔyè, ér shì qù fān wǒ shǒujī shàng de yīnyuè bōfàng lièbiǎo. Nà lǐmiàn yǒu jiāngjìn bābǎi shǒu gē, ànzhào tiānjiā shíjiān páiliè, gòuchéng le yí bù bǐ rènhé rìjì dōu gèng chéngshí de qínggǎn biānniánshǐ. Dàxué shíqī tiānjiā de gēqǔ chōngmǎn le fènnù hé lǐxiǎng zhǔyì — yáogǔnyuè, péngkè, dài yǒu shèhuì pīpàn sècǎi de mínyáo. Èrshíwǔ suì dào èrshíbā suì zhī jiān de gēdān jīhū quán shì Yīngwén gē, nà shì wǒ kèyì tuòzhǎn shěnměi biānjiè de jiēduàn. Sānshí suì yǐhòu de gēdān kāishǐ huíguī Zhōngwén gēqǔ, érqiě lèixíng zhújiàn jízhōng dào le qīng yīnyuè hé juéshì — wǒ cāi zhè dàgài jiùshì rénmen suǒ shuō de 「kǒuwèi biàn qīngdàn le」. Zuì sīmì de shì nàxiē bèi biāojì wéi 「shēnyè zhuānyòng」 de gēdān, lǐmiàn de qǔmù xuǎnzé bàolù le wǒ zài lǐxìng biǎomiàn zhī xià de cuìruò — nàxiē zài língchén liǎng diǎn dānqǔ xúnhuán bōfàng de gē, měi yì shǒu dōu duìyìng zhe yí gè bú yuànyì xiàng rènhé rén tíqǐ de shíkè. Wǒ yǒushíhou huì xiǎng, rúguǒ wǒ qùshì yǐhòu yǒu rén dǎkāi wǒ de gēdān, tāmen néng bù néng jǐnjǐn tōngguò yīnyuè de guǐjì huányuán yí gè wánzhěng de wǒ? Yěxǔ bù néng, dàn tāmen zhìshǎo néng chùmō dào nàxiē wǒ cóngwèi shuō chūkǒu de qíngxù.
text_en: If someone wanted to truly understand me, I'd suggest they skip my social media profile and scroll through the music playlist on my phone. It contains nearly eight hundred songs, arranged by date added, composing an emotional chronicle more honest than any diary. Songs added during college are full of anger and idealism — rock, punk, folk with social critique. The playlist from ages twenty-five to twenty-eight is almost entirely English songs — that was my phase of deliberately expanding aesthetic boundaries. After thirty, the playlist began returning to Chinese songs, with genres gradually concentrating on light music and jazz — I suppose this is what people call 'tastes becoming milder.' The most intimate are the playlists marked 'late night only,' whose track selection exposes the vulnerability beneath my rational surface — those songs played on single-track repeat at 2 AM, each corresponding to a moment I'm unwilling to mention to anyone. I sometimes wonder: if someone opened my playlist after I died, could they reconstruct a complete me solely through the trajectory of music? Perhaps not, but they could at least touch the emotions I never spoke aloud.

questions.q_zh: 作者三十岁以后的歌单有什么变化？
questions.q_en: How did the author's playlist change after thirty?

options.text: 回归中文歌曲，集中到轻音乐和爵士
options.pinyin: huíguī Zhōngwén gēqǔ, jízhōng dào qīng yīnyuè hé juéshì
options.text_en: Returned to Chinese songs, focusing on light music and jazz

questions.q_zh: 作者认为歌单比什么更诚实？
questions.q_en: What does the author believe the playlist is more honest than?

title: The Age I Feel Inside
text_zh: 我的身份证上写着三十八岁，但我的「内心年龄」在不同时刻呈现出完全不同的数字。工作中处理复杂项目的时候，我觉得自己至少有四十五岁——冷静、有经验、对组织政治的暗流有敏锐的直觉。但是在书店里看到一本感兴趣的书时，我的内心年龄会突然降到十六岁——那种纯粹的、不带任何功利计算的好奇心重新苏醒。周末和老朋友们聚会时喝了几杯酒以后，内心年龄变成了二十三岁，大家开始回忆大学时代的荒唐事，笑得像还没进入社会的学生。最让我困惑的是面对父母的时候：我一方面是一个照顾他们的成年人，另一方面又会在母亲的一句责备面前瞬间退化成一个八岁的孩子。也许人的年龄从来不是一个固定的数字，而是一个根据环境和关系不断浮动的光谱。那些自称「永远年轻」的人可能只是指他们在某些特定时刻能够重新连接到年轻时的感受。而我已经学会了在这个光谱的不同位置之间自如地移动——这本身，也许才是真正成熟的标志。
text_pinyin: Wǒ de shēnfènzhèng shàng xiě zhe sānshíbā suì, dàn wǒ de 「nèixīn niánlíng」 zài bùtóng shíkè chéngxiàn chū wánquán bùtóng de shùzì. Gōngzuò zhōng chǔlǐ fùzá xiàngmù de shíhou, wǒ juéde zìjǐ zhìshǎo yǒu sìshíwǔ suì — lěngjìng, yǒu jīngyàn, duì zǔzhī zhèngzhì de ànliú yǒu mǐnruì de zhíjué. Dànshì zài shūdiàn lǐ kàn dào yì běn gǎn xìngqù de shū shí, wǒ de nèixīn niánlíng huì tūrán jiàng dào shíliù suì — nà zhǒng chúncuì de, bú dài rènhé gōnglì jìsuàn de hàoqíxīn chóngxīn sūxǐng. Zhōumò hé lǎo péngyoumen jùhuì shí hē le jǐ bēi jiǔ yǐhòu, nèixīn niánlíng biàn chéng le èrshísān suì, dàjiā kāishǐ huíyì dàxué shídài de huāngtáng shì, xiào de xiàng hái méi jìnrù shèhuì de xuéshēng. Zuì ràng wǒ kùnhuò de shì miànduì fùmǔ de shíhou: wǒ yì fāngmiàn shì yí gè zhàogù tāmen de chéngnián rén, lìng yì fāngmiàn yòu huì zài mǔqīn de yí jù zébèi miànqián shùnjiān tuìhuà chéng yí gè bā suì de háizi. Yěxǔ rén de niánlíng cónglái búshì yí gè gùdìng de shùzì, ér shì yí gè gēnjù huánjìng hé guānxi búduàn fúdòng de guāngpǔ. Nàxiē zìchēng 「yǒngyuǎn niánqīng」 de rén kěnéng zhǐ shì zhǐ tāmen zài mǒuxiē tèdìng shíkè nénggòu chóngxīn liánjiē dào niánqīng shí de gǎnshòu. Ér wǒ yǐjīng xuéhuì le zài zhège guāngpǔ de bùtóng wèizhì zhī jiān zìrú de yídòng — zhè běnshēn, yěxǔ cái shì zhēnzhèng chéngshú de biāozhì.
text_en: My ID says thirty-eight, but my 'inner age' displays completely different numbers at different moments. When handling complex projects at work, I feel at least forty-five — calm, experienced, with sharp intuition for the undercurrents of organizational politics. But when I spot an interesting book at the bookstore, my inner age suddenly drops to sixteen — that pure curiosity free of any utilitarian calculation reawakens. After a few drinks at a weekend gathering with old friends, inner age becomes twenty-three, as everyone starts reminiscing about absurd college stories, laughing like students who haven't yet entered society. Most confusing is facing my parents: on one hand I'm an adult caring for them, on the other I can instantly regress to an eight-year-old before a single reproach from my mother. Perhaps a person's age was never a fixed number, but a spectrum that constantly fluctuates according to environment and relationships. Those who claim to be 'forever young' may simply mean they can reconnect with youthful feelings at certain specific moments. And I've already learned to move freely between different positions on this spectrum — this itself is perhaps the true marker of maturity.

questions.q_zh: 作者在书店里看到感兴趣的书时，内心年龄变成了多少？
questions.q_en: What does the author's inner age become when spotting an interesting book?

questions.q_zh: 作者认为真正成熟的标志是什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author consider the true marker of maturity?

options.text: 能在不同内心年龄之间自如移动
options.pinyin: néng zài bùtóng nèixīn niánlíng zhī jiān zìrú yídòng
options.text_en: Being able to move freely between different inner ages
No longer reminiscing about the past

title: The Collection That Became Me
text_zh: 搬家的时候才意识到，我的身份在很大程度上是由我收藏的东西定义的。三个书架的书——哲学、建筑学、侦探小说、日本文学——勾勒出了我精神世界的轮廓。窗台上的十二盆多肉植物记录了我学会照顾活着的东西的过程：最早买的那盆已经长到了花盆容不下的地步，最近买的还是小小的幼苗。厨房里整整一柜子的调味料和烹饪工具暗示着我对食物的态度——不是快速果腹，而是把做饭当作一种冥想式的日常仪式。抽屉里有一个专门收集音乐会票根和博物馆门票的信封，里面装着过去十年我所有文化消费的物质证据。搬家公司的人看着我的东西说：「你的箱子真多，但大件家具很少。」这句随口的评论精准地概括了我的生活哲学：比起占据空间的家具，我更看重的是那些能占据头脑的东西。当所有的箱子被搬进新公寓以后，在空荡荡的房间里把它们一件一件摆放到新的位置上时，我意识到，整理物品的过程其实就是重新组装自我的过程。每一件物品放好以后，我就重新变得完整了一点。
text_pinyin: Bānjiā de shíhou cái yìshí dào, wǒ de shēnfèn zài hěn dà chéngdù shàng shì yóu wǒ shōucáng de dōngxi dìngyì de. Sān gè shūjià de shū — zhéxué, jiànzhùxué, zhēntàn xiǎoshuō, Rìběn wénxué — gōulè chū le wǒ jīngshén shìjiè de lúnkuò. Chuāngtái shàng de shí'èr pén duōròu zhíwù jìlù le wǒ xuéhuì zhàogù huó zhe de dōngxi de guòchéng: zuì zǎo mǎi de nà pén yǐjīng zhǎng dào le huāpén róng bú xià de dìbù, zuìjìn mǎi de háishi xiǎo xiǎo de yòumiáo. Chúfáng lǐ zhěngzhěng yí guìzi de tiáowèiliào hé pēngrèn gōngjù ànshì zhe wǒ duì shíwù de tàidù — búshì kuàisù guǒ fù, ér shì bǎ zuòfàn dāng zuò yì zhǒng míngxiǎng shì de rìcháng yíshì. Chōuti lǐ yǒu yí gè zhuānmén shōují yīnyuè huì piàogēn hé bówùguǎn ménpiào de xìnfēng, lǐmiàn zhuāng zhe guòqù shí nián wǒ suǒyǒu wénhuà xiāofèi de wùzhì zhèngjù. Bānjiā gōngsī de rén kàn zhe wǒ de dōngxi shuō: 「nǐ de xiāngzi zhēn duō, dàn dàjiàn jiājù hěn shǎo.」 Zhè jù suíkǒu de pínglùn jīngzhǔn de gàikuò le wǒ de shēnghuó zhéxué: bǐ qǐ zhànjù kōngjiān de jiājù, wǒ gèng kànzhòng de shì nàxiē néng zhànjù tóunǎo de dōngxi. Dāng suǒyǒu de xiāngzi bèi bān jìn xīn gōngyù yǐhòu, zài kōngdàngdàng de fángjiān lǐ bǎ tāmen yí jiàn yí jiàn bǎi fàng dào xīn de wèizhì shàng shí, wǒ yìshí dào, zhěnglǐ wùpǐn de guòchéng qíshí jiùshì chóngxīn zǔzhuāng zìwǒ de guòchéng. Měi yí jiàn wùpǐn fàng hǎo yǐhòu, wǒ jiù chóngxīn biàn de wánzhěng le yìdiǎn.
text_en: Only when moving did I realize that my identity is largely defined by what I collect. Three bookshelves of books — philosophy, architecture, detective fiction, Japanese literature — outline the contours of my mental world. The twelve succulent plants on the windowsill record my journey of learning to care for living things: the earliest purchase has outgrown its pot, the most recent is still a tiny seedling. An entire kitchen cabinet of seasonings and cooking tools hints at my attitude toward food — not quick sustenance, but treating cooking as a meditative daily ritual. In a drawer there's an envelope specifically collecting concert stubs and museum tickets, containing the material evidence of all my cultural consumption over the past decade. The movers looked at my things and said: 'You have so many boxes, but hardly any large furniture.' This offhand comment precisely summarized my life philosophy: compared to furniture that occupies space, I value more the things that can occupy the mind. After all the boxes were carried into the new apartment, as I placed them one by one into new positions in the empty rooms, I realized that the process of organizing belongings is actually the process of reassembling the self. With each item placed, I became a little more complete again.

questions.q_zh: 搬家公司的人对作者的东西有什么评价？
questions.q_en: What did the movers comment about the author's belongings?

options.text: 箱子真多，但大件家具很少
options.pinyin: xiāngzi zhēn duō, dàn dàjiàn jiājù hěn shǎo
options.text_en: So many boxes, but hardly any large furniture

questions.q_zh: 作者认为整理物品的过程其实是什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author believe the process of organizing belongings actually is?

options.text_en: The process of reassembling the self
